title: Symphyotrichum adnatum
taxonomy: Symphyotrichum adnatum is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It belongs to the genus Symphyotrichum, which is a group of daisy-like plants native to North America. Symphyotrichum adnatum is often referred to as the bog aster or marsh aster, due to its habitat in wetland areas. The plant is a perennial that grows to be around 1-3 feet tall, with purple or pink flowers blooming in late summer. As a member of the Asteraceae family, Symphyotrichum adnatum is closely related to other daisy-like plants, such as coneflowers and black-eyed Susans.

side_effects: Symphyotrichum adnatum, also known as the prairie goldenrod, is a plant that is often mistakenly blamed for causing allergies. intro: Symphyotrichum adnatum, also known as the late goldenrod or late yellow goldenrod, is a perennial plant species native to eastern North America, characterized by its bright yellow flowers and slender stems. This plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of colds, sore throats, and digestive issues.
